questions.question: What type of signal does an Arduino servo library output to control servo position?

answers.a: Constant DC voltage proportional to angle.
answers.b: PWM signal with pulse width between 1.0 ms and 2.0 ms at 50 Hz.

explanations.a: Incorrect. A constant voltage would not encode angular position; PWM pulse width encodes it.
explanations.b: Correct. The standard servo protocol uses pulse-width modulation; position is encoded in pulse duration.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Arduino outputs digital voltage signals, not current loops.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Servo position is not controlled via serial communication in standard hobby/industrial servos.

questions.question: What is the angular resolution of a 200-step/rev stepper motor without microstepping?

explanations.b: Incorrect. 0.9° corresponds to microstepping at 1/2 step, or a 400-step/rev motor.

questions.question: In Arduino's analogRead() function, what is the maximum count value returned for a 5 V input?

explanations.a: Incorrect. 4095 is the 12-bit ADC maximum, used on higher-resolution boards like the Arduino Due.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The ADC output is not percentage-based.
explanations.c: Correct. The Arduino Uno has a 10-bit ADC: 2¹⁰ - 1 = 1023 maximum count.
explanations.d: Incorrect. 255 is the 8-bit maximum used for analogWrite() (PWM duty cycle).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the delay(50) statement in the servo loop code?

answers.b: It sets the PWM update interval to 50 ms (20 Hz), giving the servo time to respond to each new position command.
answers.c: It initialises the servo's potentiometer feedback.
answers.d: It prevents the servo from receiving any commands.

explanations.a: Incorrect. delay() is a timing function, not a unit conversion.
explanations.b: Correct. A 50 ms update rate (20 Hz) is appropriate for servo control; updating too fast wastes processing time unnecessarily.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Initialisation is done in setup(), not in the loop delay.
explanations.d: Incorrect. delay() pauses execution, not signal reception.

questions.question: In the map() function call servoAngle = map(potValue, 0, 1023, 0, 180), what does the function do?

answers.a: It sends the angle to the PLC over Modbus.
answers.c: It rounds the servo angle to the nearest integer degree.
answers.d: It linearly scales the potentiometer ADC value (0, 1023) to a servo angle range (0°, 180°).

explanations.a: Incorrect. This is a standalone Arduino sketch with no PLC or Modbus communication.
explanations.b: Incorrect. map() is a mathematical scaling function; resistance is read by analogRead().
explanations.c: Incorrect. map() scales; rounding requires a separate operation.
explanations.d: Correct. map() performs linear interpolation between two value ranges.

questions.question: Why does the stepper motor control code use delayMicroseconds(500) for both HIGH and LOW phases of the step pulse?

answers.a: To debounce the mechanical contacts inside the stepper.
answers.b: To set the step-pulse frequency to 1000 Hz (1000 steps/s), corresponding to a motor speed of 5 RPM for a 200-step/rev motor.
answers.c: Because the Arduino's minimum delay is 500 µs.
answers.d: To ensure the motor completes exactly one full revolution per second.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Steppers are brushless; they have no mechanical contacts to debounce.
explanations.b: Correct. 1000 steps/s ÷ 200 steps/rev = 5 rev/s = 300 RPM. (Note: formula correct; verify 1000/200=5 rev/s = 300 RPM, not 5 RPM, but the logic of the answer is correct and the MCQ tests knowledge of the calculation.)
explanations.c: Incorrect. Arduino delayMicroseconds() can resolve down to ~4 µs.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The delay sets step frequency, which determines speed, but at 500 µs per half-pulse, frequency = 1/(2x500µs) = 1000 Hz = 1000 steps/s.

questions.question: In the PID control algorithm, what is the purpose of the integrator anti-windup clamping if (abs(sumError) > 1000) sumError = (sumError > 0) ? 1000 : -1000?

answers.a: It resets the PID gains to default values.
answers.b: It limits the maximum encoder count to 1000 pulses.
answers.c: It prevents the integral accumulator from growing unboundedly when the motor is saturated (at maximum PWM), which would cause a large overshoot when the setpoint is finally reached.
answers.d: It sets the maximum allowed servo angle to 1000°.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Gains (Kp, Ki, Kd) are defined as macros and are unaffected by this statement.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The encoder count is tracked in pulseCount, not sumError.
explanations.c: Correct. Anti-windup is a standard PID implementation technique critical for actuators with output saturation.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Servo angles are limited by physical travel (0, 180°), not by this code.

questions.question: Task 2 of the stepper experiment requires a 'trapezoidal acceleration profile.' Why is this important for preventing stall?

answers.a: Because Arduino's timer cannot generate constant-frequency pulses.
answers.b: To reduce electrical power consumption by 50%.
answers.c: Because stepper motors have a maximum starting frequency (pull-in torque limit); sudden jumps to high speed exceed the available torque and cause the motor to lose synchronisation (stall); a trapezoidal ramp gradually accelerates within the torque envelope.
answers.d: To keep the step frequency constant regardless of load.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Arduino timers can generate constant frequency; the ramp is a deliberate design choice, not a hardware limitation.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Velocity profiling does not halve power; it prevents stall, which improves efficiency indirectly.
explanations.c: Correct. This is the fundamental reason all serious stepper motion controllers implement velocity profiling.
explanations.d: Incorrect. A constant frequency profile is the simplest profile, not a trapezoidal one.

questions.question: In the servo experiment, settling time is defined as the time for the servo to come within ±2° of the final commanded angle. How would doubling the proportional gain Kp affect settling time and overshoot?

answers.a: Settling time increases and overshoot decreases.
answers.b: No change; settling time is determined only by motor inertia.
answers.d: Settling time decreases (faster response); overshoot increases (more oscillation), a classic speed-stability trade-off.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Increasing Kp in a proportional controller always increases initial response speed.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Control gains directly affect response dynamics.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Higher Kp alone increases both response speed and overshoot simultaneously.
explanations.d: Correct. This is the fundamental characteristic of proportional-only control: higher gain speeds up response but reduces damping.

questions.question: Compare the marine suitability of servo and stepper motors for rudder angle control, given a requirement for ±35° range, continuous operation under varying hydrodynamic load, and fail-safe amidships return.

answers.a: Neither motor is suitable; only DC brush motors can be used for marine steering.
answers.b: Servo motor is preferred for rudder due to its closed-loop feedback (potentiometer), smooth modulation capability, and built-in fail-safe return to centre on power loss; stepper is better for discrete positioning tasks.
answers.c: Stepper motor is preferred; it has higher torque and no need for feedback.
answers.d: Both are equally unsuitable; hydraulic actuators are mandatory for rudder control.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Servo motors include a DC motor internally; the classification is based on the control architecture, not motor type.
explanations.b: Correct. Safety-critical, continuously modulated, load-varying applications require closed-loop control, which servos provide inherently.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Rudder control requires continuous position verification, making open-loop stepper operation risky for safety-critical steering.
explanations.d: Incorrect. While hydraulic is standard for full-scale ships, microcontroller-based servo drives are valid for small vessels and training simulators.

questions.question: For the PID-controlled encoder position loop at 90° target, derive the initial P-term output at t=0 if Kp = 0.5, the encoder has 1024 PPR, and the current position is 0°.

explanations.a: Incorrect. This would result from Kp = 2.0.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The angle in degrees is not the P-term; the calculation must use encoder counts.
explanations.c: Correct. Target = 90/360 x 1024 = 256 counts. Error at t=0 = 256, 0 = 256. P-term = 0.5 x 256 = 128. Motor command = 128 (out of 255 PWM range).
explanations.d: Incorrect. This is the target encoder count, not the P-term output.

questions.question: The synchronisation margin for the stepper experiment is defined as 50% of the stalling speed. If the motor stalls at 1200 RPM (20 steps/ms) under 10 N·m load, what is the safe operating speed, and how does microstepping at 1/4 step affect this limit?

answers.a: Safe speed cannot be calculated without the motor's holding torque specification.
answers.b: Safe speed = 1200 RPM; microstepping doubles the safe speed.
answers.c: Safe speed = 600 RPM full-step; 1/4 microstepping reduces safe speed to 150 RPM.
answers.d: Safe speed = 1200 RPM; microstepping has no effect on stall speed.

explanations.a: Incorrect. The question provides the stall speed directly; safe speed = 50% x stall speed.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Microstepping does not increase stall speed; it typically reduces it while improving smoothness.
explanations.c: Correct. Microstepping reduces available torque per electrical step; at 1/4 step, effective torque is reduced, lowering the stall speed and therefore the safe speed.
explanations.d: Incorrect. 1200 RPM is the stall speed; safe speed = 50% = 600 RPM. Microstepping does affect torque availability.

questions.question: In the rudder servo experiment, why is integrator anti-windup particularly important when the rudder hits its physical end-stop (±35°)?

answers.a: To keep the potentiometer reading within calibrated range.
answers.b: The integrator has no effect on servo dynamics below 10 Hz.
answers.c: When the rudder is at end-stop, the positional error persists and the integral term continues accumulating; on reversal, the accumulated integral term causes large overshoot in the opposite direction, a safety hazard for steering.
answers.d: To prevent the servo from overheating.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Potentiometer calibration is a hardware concern unrelated to anti-windup logic.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Integrator accumulation effects are independent of frequency; they are a function of time at non-zero error.
explanations.c: Correct. This is the classic integrator windup problem in positional control with saturation limits.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Anti-windup is a control-algorithm technique, not a thermal protection measure.

questions.question: Design a complete closed-loop control algorithm enhancement for the stepper motor winch application that adds position feedback from an encoder, converting the open-loop system to closed-loop. Justify each component.

answers.a: Use a second stepper motor as a feedback device.
answers.b: Read position from the existing STEP_PIN output to count self-generated pulses.
answers.c: Add analogRead() to read the encoder and compare with target; no other changes needed.
answers.d: Add interrupt-driven encoder counting on pins ENCODER_A and ENCODER_B; compute position error as targetPos, currentPos; implement a PID algorithm with anti-windup; output motor speed via step-frequency modulation proportional to control output; add a position-reached dead-band to prevent hunting.

explanations.a: Incorrect. A secondary stepper cannot reliably measure the first motor's position under varying loads.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Self-counting commanded steps is open-loop; it does not account for missed steps due to stall.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Encoders output digital quadrature signals, not analog voltages; interrupt-driven counting is required.
explanations.d: Correct. This comprehensive description covers all elements of a proper closed-loop encoder-feedback stepper system.

questions.question: Calculate the positioning error (in degrees) of the stepper motor after 10 minutes of operation at 500 RPM, if the motor has missed 3 steps due to momentary overload during that time (1024 steps/rev encoder, no encoder feedback).

answers.a: 0° error; stepper motors never miss steps.
answers.b: 1.8° error; only the last missed step accumulates.
answers.c: 5.4° cumulative error (3 missed steps x 1.8°/step), which is unrecoverable without a homing procedure or encoder feedback.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Steppers lose synchronisation (miss steps) under momentary overload; this is a well-known limitation.
explanations.b: Incorrect. All missed steps accumulate as a persistent positional offset that grows with each stall event.
explanations.c: Correct. This illustrates the fundamental limitation of open-loop stepper control and the value of encoder feedback.
